one_line_takeaway: Circuit-level tracing of Gemma-2-2b on 472 PrimeVul C/C++ samples shows the model detects vulnerabilities largely by absence of learned 'safety detector' attention heads rather than direct vulnerability recognition, with ablation of Layer 11 collapsing vulnerability-detection accuracy from 100% to 6% while safe-code recognition stays at 65%.
key_finding: Ablating Layer 11 alone drops True-Positive (vulnerability) detection accuracy from 100% to 6.0% (overall accuracy 100%->48.9%) while True-Negative (safe-code) accuracy only falls to 64.9%, demonstrating a strongly asymmetric 'Safety Default' failure mode. Ablating just the top-20 vulnerability-selective neurons in Layer 7 (under 0.2% of that layer's neurons) reduces TP accuracy from 100% to 50.6% (roughly a 50% relative drop) while TN stays at 94.1%. Activation patching shows the safe-to-vulnerable flip rate peaks at Layer 14 (>45% with standard steering coefficient), identified as the semantic decision hub, with clear asymmetry: injecting safety into vulnerable samples flips predictions far more easily than the reverse. Circuit sparsity: only 16.1% of probed attribution-graph nodes (1,597/9,944) are active for a representative CWE-787 sample, with node density collapsing from 414 active nodes in Layer 0 to 19 in Layer 25. Per-CWE breakdown shows concurrency vulnerabilities trigger 70% higher L0 activation than memory-safety vulnerabilities, and syntactic bugs (CWE-787, Cohen's d=1.99 at Layer 2) peak early and decay while semantic/state-tracking bugs (CWE-415 double-free, d=1.10 at Layer 14) stay active deep into the network.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is the first systematic mechanistic-interpretability study of LLM-based software vulnerability detection. Using Circuit Tracer on Gemma-2-2b (26 layers, 8 heads/layer, hidden dim 2,304) over 472 balanced C/C++ samples (236 vulnerable, 236 non-vulnerable, drawn from PrimeVul), the authors trace attention heads, MLP neurons, and attribution graphs, then causally validate their findings with layer-wise mean ablation, neuron-level ablation, attention-head knockout, and activation patching. Their central novel finding is that the model performs 'detection by absence': the top attention heads it relies on (L5-H2, L2-H2, L21-H5, L7-H6, L0-H2, all with negative Importance Scores in Table 1) function as 'safety detectors' that fire strongly on safe coding patterns (boundary checks, input validation); the model classifies code as vulnerable when these safety detectors fail to activate, rather than via heads that directly recognize vulnerability signatures. They further localize vulnerability-related feature encoding to 20 specialized MLP neurons in Layer 7 (Vulnerability Selectivity Score peaking at ~0.25) and 'decision crystallization' to Layers 10-11, and show the whole circuit is sparse: only 16.1% (1,597 of 9,944) of probed attribution-graph nodes are active for a sample CWE-787 case.
threat_model: The paper is not primarily an attack/defense paper but an interpretability study of a defensive tool (an LLM used as a vulnerability detector). It does, however, derive a concrete adversarial implication from its own findings: because the model detects vulnerabilities by the absence of learned 'safety patterns' rather than by directly recognizing exploit signatures, the authors hypothesize (Sec 5.4.3) a 'Safety Mimicry' evasion attack: an adversary could inject inert-but-syntactically-valid safety checks (e.g., irrelevant boundary validations) into malicious code to artificially activate the safety-detector heads (e.g., L5-H2) and force a false negative. This attack is proposed as a theoretical implication of the mechanistic finding; the paper does not implement, test, or evaluate the Safety Mimicry attack empirically. The authors also note in Broader Impact that exposing circuit-level internals could let adversaries target identified 'blind spots,' but again this is a discussion-section risk statement, not a demonstrated exploit.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations (Sec 5.6): all experiments use a single model, Gemma-2-2b, and circuits may differ substantially in other architectures (CodeBERT, StarCoder, GPT-based models) -- no cross-model validation is performed. The 472-sample dataset, while adequate for initial circuit discovery, is stated to limit statistical power for fine-grained per-CWE analysis (some categories, e.g., Concurrency, have only n=9 per Figure 6's own caption). Analysis is restricted to C/C++ code and only 9 CWE categories; other languages/vulnerability types are untested. The identified Layer 7/11 neurons are acknowledged as likely polysemantic, and the authors suggest sparse autoencoders as future work to decompose them into monosemantic features. The proposed 'Safety Mimicry' evasion attack is a hypothesis derived from the mechanistic findings and is explicitly not empirically tested in this paper. The paper also relies on PrimeVul's stated 86-92% label accuracy rather than independently re-verifying ground-truth labels for the 472-sample subset.

datasets_used: PrimeVul (Ding et al. 2025) -- 472-sample balanced subset (236 vulnerable / 236 non-vulnerable C/C++ functions), 9 CWE categories, dataset's own reported label accuracy 86-92%
quantitative_results: Layer 11 mean ablation: overall accuracy 100.0% -> 48.9% (Delta -51.1%), TP 100%->6.0%, TN 100%->64.9%
Layer 7 mean ablation: overall 100.0%->51.2% (Delta -48.8%), TP 100%->19.3%, TN 100%->63.1%
Layer 6 mean ablation: overall 100.0%->76.4% (Delta -23.6%), TP 100%->27.7%, TN 100%->94.6%
Layer 10 mean ablation: overall 100.0%->75.7% (Delta -24.3%), TP 100%->19.3%, TN 100%->96.9%
Top-20 Layer-7 neuron ablation (<0.2% of layer's neurons): overall 100.0%->82.3% (Delta -17.7%), TP 100%->50.6%, TN 100%->94.1%
L0-H2 attention head knockout: overall 100.0%->85.9% (Delta -14.1%), TP 100%->61.5%, TN 100%->95.1%
Attribution-graph sparsity: 16.1% of nodes active (1,597 of 9,944) for a CWE-787 buffer-overflow example
Layer 0: 414 active feature nodes (dense, syntax processing) vs Layer 25: 19 active nodes (sparse, decision)
Top attention head L5-H2 Importance Score = -0.49; L2-H2 = -0.42; L21-H5 = -0.41; L7-H6 = -0.41; L0-H2 = -0.41 (Table 1, all negative = safety detectors)
L0 norm magnitudes: vulnerable samples ~25,000-220,000 vs non-vulnerable ~5,000-15,000
KS test on L0-norm distributions: p<0.001 across all 26 layers

Cohen's d for L2 residual-stream L2-norm difference: d=1.76 (peak, Layer 2, 95% bootstrap CI [1.06, 1.42]); Layer 25: d=-0.74 (p~1e-8 after correction); Layer 2 p~1e-26; Layer 12 CI [-1.12, 1.29] spans zero
MLP Vulnerability Selectivity Score S_n peaks at ~0.25 in Layer 7
Activation patching: safe->vulnerable flip rate peaks at Layer 14, >45% with standard steering coefficient
CWE-787 (Out-of-bounds Write): d=1.99 at Layer 2 (early, syntactic)
CWE-415 (Double Free): d=1.10, peaks again at Layer 14 (deep, state-tracking)
Concurrency vulnerabilities (n=9) trigger 70% higher L0 activation than Memory Safety vulnerabilities (n=124)
baselines_compared: No external ML baselines are benchmarked in this paper -- comparisons are internal (ablated vs. 100% unablated Gemma-2-2b baseline, and vulnerable-class vs. non-vulnerable-class activation statistics on the same model).
